Claude Code Is Quietly Deleting Your Work HistorySession transcripts vanish after 30 days by default, with no warning and no recovery. Here’s what you’re losing, how to stop it, and how to keep the part that matters.
At the end of June, The Register ran a story about Claude Code users discovering that their chat records were being mysteriously wiped. The GitHub issues behind it make for uncomfortable reading: developers going back to review a past session, or resume an old conversation, and finding it simply gone. No warning, no opt-in, no recovery.
It isn’t a bug. Claude Code ships with a retention setting called cleanupPeriodDays, and by default it deletes local session transcripts older than 30 days every time it starts up. The behavior is documented, technically. But almost nobody reads retention defaults, so for most people the first sign is an old session that no longer exists.
If you use Claude Code every day, it’s worth understanding what’s actually in those files, because they’re more valuable than they look.
What’s in a session transcript
Every Claude Code session is written to a JSONL file under ~/.claude/projects/, organized by project directory. Each one contains your prompts, the model's responses, the files it read, the commands it ran and their output, plus the metadata: which model, how many input and output tokens, cache reads and writes, timestamps.
That makes a transcript three things at once. It’s a resumable conversation, the thing claude --resume needs. It's a record of how a piece of work actually got done, which is occasionally priceless when you're trying to remember why a change was made. And it's your only usage ledger, because if you're on a Pro or Max subscription, these local files are the only place your consumption is recorded in any detail. Anthropic's dashboard shows subscription users nothing.
One more thing worth knowing while we’re here: these files are plaintext. If a session read your .env or a command printed a credential, that value is sitting on disk protected only by file permissions. Security folks have started paying attention to this. It's a reason to care about what's in ~/.claude, not a reason to panic, but you should know it's there.
The 30-day guillotine
All three of those uses share the same fate: on day 31, the file is deleted at startup. Your resumable sessions, your work record, your usage ledger. Gone together.
The fix for the transcripts themselves is one line. In ~/.claude/settings.json:
{ "cleanupPeriodDays": 365 }
Set it long, or set it very long, and Claude Code will stop reaping your history. Disk is cheap and JSONL compresses well; a year of heavy use is a rounding error on a modern laptop. If you take one practical thing from this post, make it that setting.
Keeping the ledger even when the logs go
The transcripts are only half the problem, though. Even with a long retention window, log files are fragile. Tools change their formats, directories get cleaned during reinstalls, and Codex, Cursor, and the other tools you probably also use each apply their own retention whims to their own folders.
This is one of the quieter reasons I built AI Usage Tracker the way I did. It’s a free, open source macOS app that scans the local logs of ten-plus AI coding tools, Claude and Codex both, and turns them into a cost dashboard. But it doesn’t just read the logs each time. It keeps its own cache of every session it has ever seen, and when a source file disappears, the usage record survives in the cache. The conversation itself can’t be resurrected once the transcript is deleted, nothing can do that, but the ledger part, what you used, when, on which project, at what cost, persists.
In practice that means if you start running it now, your usage history stops evaporating now, whatever the retention settings of the individual tools decide to do later. Mine goes back further than any of my source logs do, which is exactly the point.
And everything stays local. No cloud, no account, no telemetry. Given that this whole post is about sensitive plaintext files on your disk, the last thing you want is a tool that uploads them somewhere.
Three minutes of homework
So, the short version. Add cleanupPeriodDays to your Claude Code settings today, because the default will eventually delete something you wanted. Be conscious that your transcripts are plaintext and treat ~/.claude accordingly. And if you want your usage history to outlive every tool's retention policy, grab AI Usage Tracker at usages.pro, free to build from source, or $9 one-time for the signed macOS build.
Your AI coding history is a real work artifact now, the same way your git history is. It’s strange how casually the tools throw it away. You don’t have to let them.
